questions.prompt: What is the most consequential *practical* insight of the Interface Segregation Principle?

options.text: Interfaces belong to the clients that call them — they should express what a consumer needs, not everything an implementation offers.
Interfaces should contain at most three methods.
Every class should implement at least one interface.
Interfaces should be placed in a separate 'interfaces' package.

questions.explanation: ISP's ownership insight is what makes it actionable: design contracts from the consumption side (a password-reset flow needs read+save, so it depends on `UserReader`/`UserWriter`), and one implementation can satisfy many slices. (b) numeric limits are ISP theater — slice along client roles, not method counts. (c) and (d) are cargo-cult conventions unrelated to the principle.

questions.prompt: A `PasswordResetService` depends on a seven-method `UserRepository` but calls only `findById` and `save`. Which costs is it actually paying? Select all that apply.

options.text: Its tests require stubbing or ignoring five irrelevant methods.
Its type signature overstates its true dependencies, hiding that it never deletes or bulk-imports.
It recompiles / re-verifies when any of the seven signatures change, including ones it never calls.
It runs slower at runtime because unused methods occupy the vtable.

questions.explanation: (a) fat mocks are the daily tax. (b) is the subtle one — a reviewer or security auditor cannot tell from types that this flow can't delete users; narrow interfaces are documentation the compiler checks. (c) over-broad build/change coupling is ISP's original motivation. (d) is false: unused interface methods have no per-call runtime cost — the damage is at design/build time, not execution time.

questions.prompt: Which detail determines whether an `OrderService` → `OrderStore` → `PostgresOrderStore` design actually follows the Dependency Inversion Principle?

options.text: Whether `OrderStore` is defined in (and owned by) the domain layer in domain vocabulary, with the Postgres adapter importing the domain — never the reverse.
Whether a DI framework (Spring, Dagger, get_it) constructs the objects.
Whether `OrderStore` has more than one implementation in production.
Whether `OrderService` receives its dependencies via constructor rather than field injection.

questions.explanation: DIP is about *ownership and direction*: the high-level policy defines the contract; details implement it. If `OrderStore` lives in the persistence package or speaks SQL vocabulary, the arrows still point the wrong way regardless of tooling. (b) and (d) are dependency *injection* — wiring mechanics that can exist without inversion. (c) a test double (in-memory store) is a perfectly sufficient second implementation; production plurality isn't required.

questions.prompt: This interface lives in the domain package. What should a reviewer flag?

options.text: The abstraction is fictional: transaction control, batching, and index hints are persistence vocabulary, so the domain still depends on database concepts — DIP in name only.
The interface should be an abstract class to allow shared logic.
beginTransaction/commit should be combined into one method to reduce the method count.
The interface is fine: it lives in the domain package, which is what DIP requires.

questions.explanation: Package placement isn't the test — vocabulary is. A domain that must call `hintIndex` cannot swap to an in-memory store or a document DB, so the 'inversion' bought nothing; this is Postgres wearing a fake mustache. The fix is a contract in domain terms (`save(Order)`, perhaps a `transactionally(Runnable)` policy hook) with batching/hints buried in the adapter. (b) and (c) rearrange the same leak. (d) mistakes the letter for the substance.

questions.prompt: Where is introducing an abstraction/interface **least** justified?

options.text: Between two classes that collaborate privately inside one cohesive module, with no I/O and no variation in sight.
Between the domain and the payment provider's SDK.
Between services and the system clock, when logic depends on 'now'.
Between the domain and the persistence layer.

questions.explanation: DIP guards *boundaries* — I/O, vendors, volatile axes. Inside a cohesive module, an interface between private collaborators is pure ceremony: no second implementation will exist, and the indirection just obscures the call graph. (b) and (d) are the canonical boundaries. (c) `Clock` is the classic testability inversion — time is I/O in disguise.

questions.prompt: How do ISP violations *cause* LSP violations?

options.text: A too-wide interface forces implementations that can't honestly support every method, so they resort to `UnsupportedOperationException`, null returns, or silent no-ops — all contract breaks.
Segregated interfaces cannot be checked by the compiler, so behavioral bugs increase.
They don't — ISP and LSP concern unrelated layers of design.
Wide interfaces slow down dispatch, and timeouts violate postconditions.

questions.explanation: The chapter-4 smell (implementations throwing 'unsupported') is usually a chapter-6 disease: the interface promises a union of all clients' needs, and each implementation can only honestly deliver a subset. Narrowing the contract to client-shaped slices lets every implementation keep every promise. (b) is backwards. (c) misses that SOLID principles interlock. (d) is nonsense dressed in plausible words.
